challenge.question: An aquatic plant produced no gas at 45 °C. Explain why no gas was produced by the plant at this temperature.
challenge.learningGoal: Connect excessive temperature to enzyme denaturation, active-site shape, failed substrate binding, and a lower reaction rate.
challenge.correctAnswer: At 45 °C the enzymes denature. Their active sites change shape, so substrates no longer fit and the photosynthesis reactions stop.

evidence.text: Enzymes in the plant or chloroplast become destroyed or denatured.
The active site changes shape, so the substrate no longer fits or binds.
At the high tested temperature no gas was produced by the aquatic plant.

evidence.source: Curated challenge transfer for AQA 84611H November 2020 question 01.8
evidence.text: Above the optimum, enzyme denaturation can outweigh the collision-rate effect, so the reaction rate falls.

illustration.title: WHEN HEAT CHANGES AN ENZYME
illustration.altText: Four stages show excessive heat denaturing an enzyme, changing its active site so the substrate no longer fits and the reaction rate falls.
illustration.caption: Too hot → enzyme denatures → active site changes → reaction rate falls.
illustration.approvedMeaning: Above the optimum, heat denatures the enzyme; its active site changes shape, the substrate no longer fits, and the reaction rate falls.
illustration.visualAnchor: One enzyme followed from a working active site to a heat-changed non-fitting shape.
illustration.continuousPath: Follow the orange heat cue from plant cell to enzyme shape, failed substrate fit, and reduced product.
illustration.textHiddenSummary: Excess heat changes the enzyme shape, prevents substrate binding, and reduces product formation.

steps.heading: TOO HOT
steps.visual: Heat enters an aquatic plant cell and chloroplast; a working enzyme and fitting substrate establish the starting state.
steps.textHiddenMeaning: The plant's enzyme-controlled process is exposed to excessive heat.
misconceptionGuards: Do not imply that one exact temperature is the optimum for every enzyme.
Do not imply that heat removes substrate particles.

steps.heading: DENATURED
steps.visual: The same enzyme changes from a fitted blue form into a visibly altered purple form under heat.
steps.textHiddenMeaning: Excess heat changes the enzyme's three-dimensional shape.
misconceptionGuards: Do not show the enzyme evaporating, dying, or disappearing.

steps.heading: ACTIVE SITE CHANGED
steps.visual: A close-up compares the altered active-site cavity with the unchanged substrate silhouette that can no longer bind.
steps.textHiddenMeaning: The changed active site and unchanged substrate no longer match.
misconceptionGuards: Do not show the substrate denaturing.
Do not suggest that fewer collisions cause the high-temperature fall.

steps.heading: REACTION RATE FALLS
steps.visual: Fewer successful enzyme-substrate complexes lead back to the aquatic plant producing less visible gas.
steps.distinctVisualAnchor: Reduced enzyme binding and gas output
steps.textHiddenMeaning: Failed binding means fewer successful reactions and less product.
misconceptionGuards: Do not show all molecules stopping their motion.
Do not add an exact universal temperature cutoff.

promptConstraints: Use the project's luminous scientific-atlas visual language and preserve a left-to-right causal scan.
Treat the chloroplast and gas output as the question context, not as extra causal stages.
Use no numerical temperature, worked value, logo, watermark, or exam-board branding.
